comment_text.sn22.2:1.1: This discourse contains Sāriputta’s advice for Buddhists teaching Dhamma in a strange land. He shows a method of giving a small amount of knowledge at a time, enough to digest and to stimulate further questioning. Indeed, he takes it for granted that the process is led by the student’s curiosity, rather than by missionary zeal for conversion.
comment_text.sn22.2:1.2: Devadaha also appears in MN 101 and SN 35.134. The name is explained in the commentary as “royal lake”, taking deva- as a term for kings, and -daha as equivalent to the Sanskrit hrada, “lake”. However it is spelled devadṛśa in the Mūlasarvāstivāda Vinaya (San Mu Kd 17:16). Dṛśa means “sight, appearance”, thus occupying a completely different semantic space than “lake”. It is a Vedic term that commonly describes Agni’s fiery gleam (Rig Veda 3.17.4, 6.10.4, 7.1.1, etc.). This suggests a Pali derivation from √ḍah, “to burn”. This in turn recalls Śatapatha Brāhmaṇa 1.4.1.14, which allegorically depicts the spread of Aryan culture eastward as Agni “burning” (dadāha) the lands, i.e. introducing the civilized practice of Vedic fire worship. I think the meaning of devadaha is, therefore, “burned by the god”, or “place of the god’s flame”. It became famous in later Buddhism as the birthplace of the sisters Māyā and Mahāpajāpatī of the Koliyan clan, the Buddha’s birth mother and foster mother. These details are not mentioned in early texts, however, where the town is said to be Sakyan rather than Koliyan. It is identified with Devdaha in the Rupandehi District of Nepal.
comment_text.sn22.2:1.4: The “western land” (pacchābhūma) is not identified, but presumably lay to the west of Verañjā (MN 42:2.1) or Kuru. See SN 42.6:2.1 and note for the “brahmins of the western land”.
comment_text.sn22.2:3.1: Eḷagalā is sometimes identified with cassia tora, but that is an introduced shrub. Perhaps it is cassia fistula (Sanskrit āragvadha), a smallish tree, native to India, with a glorious crown of golden flowers. It is the national tree of Thailand and frequently adorns temples in Sri Lanka.
comment_text.sn22.2:4.1: “Abroad” is nānāverajja, “dominion of a different king”.
comment_text.sn22.2:7.6: The aggregates are presented under the assumption that someone knowing nothing about Buddhism would understand them.
comment_text.sn22.2:7.8: Sanskrit saṁjñā came to be used in a wide variety of senses, but the pre-Buddhist usage is quite restricted. The basic meaning is “agreement, concord, mutual understanding” (Rig Veda 10.19.4, Atharva Veda 7.52.1, Śathapatha Brāhmaṇa 1.1.4.5), from which we have the causative sense in Pali, “persuade, reach an agreement with” (DN 29:18.8, Ud 2.8:13.1, Bu NP 10:1.1.14). This becomes an unsettling euphemism in the Śatapatha Brāhmaṇa, where the sacrificial beast is “persuaded” or “made to consent”, for “when they make the victim consent, they kill it” (ŚB 13.2.8.2). There seems no real precedent for the Buddhist sense of “perception” until Yājñavalkya at Bṛhadāraṇyaka Upaniṣad 2.4.12 and 4.5.13, to which DN 9 appears as a direct response. That passage contrasts the “limited” knowing of saṁjñā with the “infinite” knowing of vijñāna. Vijñāna likewise has little precedent; previously, it just meant knowing or understanding. Most likely both terms were established as an artificial pair by Yājñavalkya due to their contrasting prefixes. This led to the Buddhist idea of “perception” as a limited, conventional mode of knowing or recognizing, a usage also found in the Jain Tattvārthasūtra 1.13.
comment_text.sn22.2:10.1: Good and evil are consequential, not absolute.

root_text.sn22.2:1.2: ekaṁ samayaṁ bhagavā sakkesu viharati devadahaṁ nāma sakyānaṁ nigamo.
root_text.sn22.2:1.3: Atha kho sambahulā pacchābhūmagamikā bhikkhū yena bhagavā tenupasaṅkamiṁsu; upasaṅkamitvā bhagavantaṁ abhivādetvā ekamantaṁ nisīdiṁsu. Ekamantaṁ nisinnā kho te bhikkhū bhagavantaṁ etadavocuṁ:
root_text.sn22.2:1.4: “icchāma mayaṁ, bhante, pacchābhūmaṁ janapadaṁ gantuṁ, pacchābhūme janapade nivāsaṁ kappetun”ti.
root_text.sn22.2:2.2: “Na kho no, bhante, apalokito āyasmā sāriputto”ti.
root_text.sn22.2:2.4: Sāriputto, bhikkhave, paṇḍito, bhikkhūnaṁ anuggāhako sabrahmacārīnan”ti.
root_text.sn22.2:2.5: “Evaṁ, bhante”ti kho te bhikkhū bhagavato paccassosuṁ.
root_text.sn22.2:3.1: Tena kho pana samayena āyasmā sāriputto bhagavato avidūre aññatarasmiṁ eḷagalāgumbe nisinno hoti.
root_text.sn22.2:3.2: Atha kho te bhikkhū bhagavato bhāsitaṁ abhinanditvā anumoditvā uṭṭhāyāsanā bhagavantaṁ abhivādetvā padakkhiṇaṁ katvā yenāyasmā sāriputto tenupasaṅkamiṁsu; upasaṅkamitvā āyasmatā sāriputtena saddhiṁ sammodiṁsu.
root_text.sn22.2:3.3: Sammodanīyaṁ kathaṁ sāraṇīyaṁ vītisāretvā ekamantaṁ nisīdiṁsu. Ekamantaṁ nisinnā kho te bhikkhū āyasmantaṁ sāriputtaṁ etadavocuṁ:
root_text.sn22.2:3.4: “icchāma mayaṁ, āvuso sāriputta, pacchābhūmaṁ janapadaṁ gantuṁ, pacchābhūme janapade nivāsaṁ kappetuṁ.
root_text.sn22.2:4.4: ‘kiṁvādī panāyasmantānaṁ satthā kimakkhāyī’ti, kacci vo āyasmantānaṁ dhammā sussutā suggahitā sumanasikatā sūpadhāritā suppaṭividdhā paññāya, yathā byākaramānā āyasmanto vuttavādino ceva bhagavato assatha, na ca bhagavantaṁ abhūtena abbhācikkheyyātha, dhammassa cānudhammaṁ byākareyyātha, na ca koci sahadhammiko vādānuvādo gārayhaṁ ṭhānaṁ āgaccheyyā”ti?
root_text.sn22.2:5.1: “Dūratopi kho mayaṁ, āvuso, āgaccheyyāma āyasmato sāriputtassa santike etassa bhāsitassa atthamaññātuṁ.
root_text.sn22.2:5.2: Sādhu vatāyasmantaṁyeva sāriputtaṁ paṭibhātu etassa bhāsitassa attho”ti.
root_text.sn22.2:5.3: “Tena hāvuso, suṇātha, sādhukaṁ manasi karotha, bhāsissāmī”ti.
root_text.sn22.2:5.4: “Evamāvuso”ti kho te bhikkhū āyasmato sāriputtassa paccassosuṁ.
root_text.sn22.2:6.5: Evaṁ puṭṭhā tumhe, āvuso, evaṁ byākareyyātha:
root_text.sn22.2:7.1: Evaṁ byākatepi kho, āvuso, assuyeva uttariṁ pañhaṁ pucchitāro—
root_text.sn22.2:7.5: Evaṁ puṭṭhā tumhe, āvuso, evaṁ byākareyyātha:
root_text.sn22.2:8.1: Evaṁ byākatepi kho, āvuso, assuyeva uttariṁ pañhaṁ pucchitāro—
root_text.sn22.2:8.4: ‘kiṁ panāyasmantānaṁ ādīnavaṁ disvā rūpe chandarāgavinayakkhāyī satthā,
root_text.sn22.2:8.9: Evaṁ puṭṭhā tumhe, āvuso, evaṁ byākareyyātha:
root_text.sn22.2:8.10: ‘rūpe kho, āvuso, avigatarāgassa avigatacchandassa avigatapemassa avigatapipāsassa avigatapariḷāhassa avigatataṇhassa tassa rūpassa vipariṇāmaññathābhāvā uppajjanti sokaparidevadukkhadomanassupāyāsā.
root_text.sn22.2:8.14: avigatataṇhassa tesaṁ saṅkhārānaṁ vipariṇāmaññathābhāvā uppajjanti sokaparidevadukkhadomanassupāyāsā.
root_text.sn22.2:8.15: Viññāṇe avigatarāgassa avigatacchandassa avigatapemassa avigatapipāsassa avigatapariḷāhassa avigatataṇhassa tassa viññāṇassa vipariṇāmaññathābhāvā uppajjanti sokaparidevadukkhadomanassupāyāsā.
root_text.sn22.2:8.16: Idaṁ kho no, āvuso, ādīnavaṁ disvā rūpe chandarāgavinayakkhāyī satthā,
root_text.sn22.2:9.1: Evaṁ byākatepi kho, āvuso, assuyeva uttariṁ pañhaṁ pucchitāro—
root_text.sn22.2:9.4: ‘kiṁ panāyasmantānaṁ ānisaṁsaṁ disvā rūpe chandarāgavinayakkhāyī satthā,
root_text.sn22.2:9.9: Evaṁ puṭṭhā tumhe, āvuso, evaṁ byākareyyātha:
root_text.sn22.2:9.10: ‘rūpe kho, āvuso, vigatarāgassa vigatacchandassa vigatapemassa vigatapipāsassa vigatapariḷāhassa vigatataṇhassa tassa rūpassa vipariṇāmaññathābhāvā nuppajjanti sokaparidevadukkhadomanassupāyāsā.
root_text.sn22.2:9.13: saṅkhāresu vigatarāgassa vigatacchandassa vigatapemassa vigatapipāsassa vigatapariḷāhassa vigatataṇhassa tesaṁ saṅkhārānaṁ vipariṇāmaññathābhāvā nuppajjanti sokaparidevadukkhadomanassupāyāsā.
root_text.sn22.2:9.14: Viññāṇe vigatarāgassa vigatacchandassa vigatapemassa vigatapipāsassa vigatapariḷāhassa vigatataṇhassa tassa viññāṇassa vipariṇāmaññathābhāvā nuppajjanti sokaparidevadukkhadomanassupāyāsā.
root_text.sn22.2:9.15: Idaṁ kho no, āvuso, ānisaṁsaṁ disvā rūpe chandarāgavinayakkhāyī satthā, vedanāya …
root_text.sn22.2:10.1: Akusale cāvuso, dhamme upasampajja viharato diṭṭhe ceva dhamme sukho vihāro abhavissa avighāto anupāyāso apariḷāho, kāyassa ca bhedā paraṁ maraṇā sugati pāṭikaṅkhā, nayidaṁ bhagavā akusalānaṁ dhammānaṁ pahānaṁ vaṇṇeyya.
root_text.sn22.2:10.2: Yasmā ca kho, āvuso, akusale dhamme upasampajja viharato diṭṭhe ceva dhamme dukkho vihāro savighāto saupāyāso sapariḷāho, kāyassa ca bhedā paraṁ maraṇā duggati pāṭikaṅkhā, tasmā bhagavā akusalānaṁ dhammānaṁ pahānaṁ vaṇṇeti.
root_text.sn22.2:11.1: Kusale cāvuso, dhamme upasampajja viharato diṭṭhe ceva dhamme dukkho vihāro abhavissa savighāto saupāyāso sapariḷāho, kāyassa ca bhedā paraṁ maraṇā duggati pāṭikaṅkhā, nayidaṁ bhagavā kusalānaṁ dhammānaṁ upasampadaṁ vaṇṇeyya.
root_text.sn22.2:11.2: Yasmā ca kho, āvuso, kusale dhamme upasampajja viharato diṭṭhe ceva dhamme sukho vihāro avighāto anupāyāso apariḷāho, kāyassa ca bhedā paraṁ maraṇā sugati pāṭikaṅkhā, tasmā bhagavā kusalānaṁ dhammānaṁ upasampadaṁ vaṇṇetī”ti.
root_text.sn22.2:12.2: Attamanā te bhikkhū āyasmato sāriputtassa bhāsitaṁ abhinandunti.

translation_text.sn22.2:1.2: At one time the Buddha was staying in the land of the Sakyans, where they have a town named Devadaha.
translation_text.sn22.2:1.3: Then several mendicants who were heading for the west went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him,
translation_text.sn22.2:1.4: “Sir, we wish to go to a western land to take up residence there.”
translation_text.sn22.2:2.1: “But mendicants, have you taken leave of Sāriputta?”
translation_text.sn22.2:2.4: He’s astute, and supports his spiritual companions, the mendicants.”
translation_text.sn22.2:3.1: Now at that time Venerable Sāriputta was sitting not far from the Buddha in a clump of golden shower trees.
translation_text.sn22.2:3.2: And then those mendicants approved and agreed with what the Buddha said. They got up from their seat, bowed, and respectfully circled the Buddha, keeping him on their right. Then they went up to Venerable Sāriputta, and exchanged greetings with him.
translation_text.sn22.2:3.3: When the greetings and polite conversation were over, they sat down to one side and said to him,
translation_text.sn22.2:3.4: “Reverend Sāriputta, we wish to go to a western land to take up residence there.
translation_text.sn22.2:3.5: We have taken leave of the Teacher.”
translation_text.sn22.2:4.1: “Reverends, there are those who question a mendicant who has gone abroad—
translation_text.sn22.2:4.2: astute aristocrats, brahmins, householders, and ascetics—
translation_text.sn22.2:4.4: ‘But what does the venerables’ Teacher teach? What does he explain?’ I trust the venerables have properly heard, learned, applied the mind, and remembered the teachings, and penetrated them with wisdom. That way, when answering you will repeat what the Buddha has said and not misrepresent him with an untruth. You will explain in line with the teaching, with no legitimate grounds for rebuttal and criticism.”
translation_text.sn22.2:5.1: “Reverend, we would travel a long way to learn the meaning of this statement in the presence of Venerable Sāriputta.
translation_text.sn22.2:5.2: May Venerable Sāriputta himself please clarify the meaning of this.”
translation_text.sn22.2:5.3: “Well then, reverends, listen and apply your mind well, I will speak.”
translation_text.sn22.2:6.1: “Reverends, there are those who question a mendicant who has gone abroad—
translation_text.sn22.2:6.2: astute aristocrats, brahmins, householders, and ascetics—
translation_text.sn22.2:6.4: ‘But what does the venerables’ Teacher teach? What does he explain?’
translation_text.sn22.2:6.5: When questioned like this, reverends, you should answer:
translation_text.sn22.2:6.6: ‘Reverend, our Teacher explained the removal of desire and lust.’
translation_text.sn22.2:7.1: When you answer like this, such astute people may inquire further:
translation_text.sn22.2:7.4: ‘But regarding what does the venerables’ teacher explain the removal of desire and lust?’
translation_text.sn22.2:7.5: When questioned like this, reverends, you should answer:
translation_text.sn22.2:7.6: ‘Our teacher explains the removal of desire and lust for form,
translation_text.sn22.2:8.1: When you answer like this, such astute people may inquire further:
translation_text.sn22.2:8.4: ‘But what drawback has he seen that he teaches the removal of desire and lust for form,
translation_text.sn22.2:8.9: When questioned like this, reverends, you should answer:
translation_text.sn22.2:8.10: ‘If you’re not free of greed, desire, fondness, thirst, passion, and craving for form, when that form decays and perishes it gives rise to sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress.
translation_text.sn22.2:8.11: If you’re not free of greed, desire, fondness, thirst, passion, and craving for feeling …
translation_text.sn22.2:8.15: consciousness, when that consciousness decays and perishes it gives rise to sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress.
translation_text.sn22.2:8.16: This is the drawback our Teacher has seen that he teaches the removal of desire and lust for form,
translation_text.sn22.2:9.1: When you answer like this, such astute people may inquire further:
translation_text.sn22.2:9.4: ‘But what benefit has he seen that he teaches the removal of desire and lust for form,
translation_text.sn22.2:9.9: When questioned like this, reverends, you should answer:
translation_text.sn22.2:9.10: ‘If you are rid of greed, desire, fondness, thirst, passion, and craving for form, when that form decays and perishes it doesn’t give rise to sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress.
translation_text.sn22.2:9.11: If you are rid of greed, desire, fondness, thirst, passion, and craving for feeling …
translation_text.sn22.2:9.14: consciousness, when that consciousness decays and perishes it doesn’t give rise to sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress.
translation_text.sn22.2:9.15: This is the benefit our Teacher has seen that he teaches the removal of desire and lust for form, feeling,
translation_text.sn22.2:10.1: If those who acquired and kept unskillful qualities were to live happily in the present life, free of distress, anguish, and fever; and if, when their body breaks up, after death, they could expect to go to a good place, the Buddha would not praise giving up unskillful qualities.
translation_text.sn22.2:10.2: But since those who acquire and keep unskillful qualities live unhappily in the present life, full of distress, anguish, and fever; and since, when their body breaks up, after death, they can expect to go to a bad place, the Buddha praises giving up unskillful qualities.
translation_text.sn22.2:11.1: If those who embraced and kept skillful qualities were to live unhappily in the present life, full of distress, anguish, and fever; and if, when their body breaks up, after death, they could expect to go to a bad place, the Buddha would not praise embracing skillful qualities.
translation_text.sn22.2:11.2: But since those who embrace and keep skillful qualities live happily in the present life, free of distress, anguish, and fever; and since, when their body breaks up, after death, they can expect to go to a good place, the Buddha praises embracing skillful qualities.”
translation_text.sn22.2:12.1: This is what Venerable Sāriputta said.
translation_text.sn22.2:12.2: Satisfied, the mendicants approved what Sāriputta said.

variant_text.sn22.2:4.4: panāyasmantānaṁ → kiṁvādāyasmantānaṁ (sya-all, pts1ed, mr) | vādānuvādo → vādānupāto (bj, sya-all aṭṭhakathāyaṁ pāṭhantaraṁ)
